def_en: the subject matter of a conversation or discussion
something (a person or object or scene) selected by an artist or photographer for graphic representation
(grammar) one of the two main constituents of a sentence; the grammatical constituent about which something is predicated
a person who is subjected to experimental or other observational procedures; someone who is an object of investigation
def_zh: 科目, 主题, 臣民, 主语, 题目, (事物的)经受者, 学科, 受治疗者, 原因, 理由
examples: The subject of the book is the history of the country.
The new policy will subject all employees to a background check.

def_zh: 服从的, 易患...的, 隶属的, 受支配的
examples: The subject of the painting is a beautiful landscape.
The new employee is subject to a three-month probation period.

gloss_en: a person or thing that is being dealt with or discussed

def_en: (usually plural) a polite expression of desire for someone's welfare
a feeling of friendship and esteem
def_zh: 关心, 注意, 尊敬, 关系, 问候
examples: He spoke of the matter with great regard for the feelings of those involved.

def_en: look at attentively
def_zh: 视为, 注意, 考虑, 和...有关, 看待
examples: She is highly regarded by her colleagues for her dedication and hard work.

gloss_en: to consider or think of in a specified way

def_en: nonfictional prose forming an independent part of a publication
one of a class of artifacts
a separate section of a legal document (as a statute or contract or will)
(grammar) a determiner that may indicate the specificity of reference of a noun phrase
def_zh: 文章, 冠词, 物品, 物件, 条款, 契约
examples: I read an interesting article about the future of artificial intelligence.

gloss_en: A piece of writing on a particular subject in a newspaper or magazine, or on a website.

term: stimulate one's interest in doing sth

def_zh: 激发某人做某事的兴趣
examples: The hands-on experiment was designed to stimulate students' interest in pursuing chemistry further.
collocations: stimulate one's interest in doing sth
stimulate my interest in the subject

gloss_en: To make someone become interested in doing something.

examples: The class was blindsided by the pop quiz because the professor had never given one before.

gloss_en: To be caught completely off guard by something unexpected.

term: oppose the university's plan to do sth

def_zh: 反对大学做某事的计划
examples: Many students oppose the university's plan to close the late-night study room during exam week.
collocations: oppose the university's plan to do sth
students oppose the plan to build

gloss_en: To disagree with and try to prevent something the university intends to do.

def_en: the act of improving by renewing and restoring
the state of being restored to its former good condition
def_zh: 革新, 刷新, 修理
examples: The old theatre reopened last month after a two-year renovation.

gloss_en: the act of repairing and improving something old, especially a building.

examples: The construction noise outside really interferes with my ability to concentrate in the reading room.

gloss_en: To disrupt, obstruct, or get in the way of something.

examples: The advisor said she wouldn't interfere in how each student manages their own project schedule.

gloss_en: To involve oneself in a situation or matter that concerns other people, usually without being asked.

def_zh: 改变事态的机会；回旋余地
examples: With the deadline fixed, the research team had little room for maneuver if their first experiment failed.

gloss_en: The freedom or flexibility to change one's plans or actions.

def_en: a military training exercise
a deliberate coordinated movement requiring dexterity and skill
a move made to gain a tactical end
def_zh: 演习, 调遣, 策略
examples: The pilot performed a skilful maneuver to avoid the approaching storm clouds.

def_zh: 调遣, 演习, 用计
examples: She carefully maneuvered the large truck into the narrow parking space.

gloss_en: a carefully planned movement or clever action intended to gain an advantage.

examples: He carried his notes in a battered old backpack that had survived four years of college.

gloss_en: Worn out and damaged from long or rough use.

examples: Let's meet in the student lounge after class; there are comfortable chairs and a coffee machine there.

gloss_en: A room on campus where students can relax, socialize, or study informally.

examples: The new recreation center has a pool and a climbing wall that all enrolled students can use for free.

gloss_en: A campus building with facilities for sports, exercise, and leisure activities.

examples: For his economics project, he interviewed a graduate who now runs a small business downtown.

gloss_en: To manage and operate a commercial enterprise.

def_zh: 为员工制定工作时间表/排班
examples: Part of the manager's job is to make up the staff schedule so the café is covered every weekend.

gloss_en: To prepare a plan showing when each employee is due to work.

examples: The facilities team is responsible for maintaining the company building, from the elevators to the heating system.
collocations: maintain the building in good condition

gloss_en: To keep the company's premises in good working condition.

examples: Each carrel in the library has its own reading lamp so you can work without disturbing your neighbor.

gloss_en: A small lamp that provides focused light for reading.

def_zh: 实时清点库存；追踪库存
examples: The bookstore uses a barcode system to track inventory so it never runs out of required textbooks.

gloss_en: To keep an ongoing record of the goods or supplies a business has in stock.

examples: The lab technician has to order supplies weeks in advance because some chemicals take a long time to ship.

gloss_en: To request and purchase the materials or goods a business needs.

examples: The shuttle makes a round trip to the airport every hour, so you won't have to wait long for a ride back.

gloss_en: A journey to a place and back again.

examples: The dining hall introduced a more nutritious menu with fresh vegetables and whole grains at every meal.

gloss_en: A set of food choices that provide good nourishment.

examples: The health lecture warned that skipping breakfast is a poor eating habit that can hurt concentration in morning classes.

gloss_en: An unhealthy pattern of eating.

examples: Being a poor swimmer, she signed up for the beginner class at the recreation center pool.

gloss_en: A person who is not good at swimming.

examples: The professor has been in poor health this term, so a substitute will cover the last two lectures.

gloss_en: To be physically unwell or in a weak state of health.

examples: In soil poor in nutrients, only hardy species like lichens can establish themselves at first.

gloss_en: Soil that lacks the substances plants need to grow well.

examples: After weeks of practice, she could finally play every right note in the difficult passage.

gloss_en: To produce the correct musical tone; figuratively, to do or say exactly the appropriate thing.

examples: I happened to overhear two colleagues discussing the merger while waiting for the lift.

def_en: v hear, usually without the knowledge of the speakers

gloss_en: to hear what other people are saying without them knowing or intending you to hear.

examples: Without the full data, the researcher could only make an educated guess about the population's size.

gloss_en: A guess based on some knowledge or evidence rather than pure chance.

examples: The professor argued that this tidy definition conceals a host of paradoxes that scholars still debate.

gloss_en: To hide a large number of contradictions beneath a seemingly simple surface.

term: strain every nerve to do sth

examples: The team strained every nerve to finish the prototype before the science fair deadline.
collocations: strain every nerve to do sth
strain every nerve to meet the deadline

gloss_en: To make every possible effort to do something.

examples: The advisor's clear explanation set the student's doubts at rest about switching majors.

gloss_en: To remove someone's uncertainties or worries about something.

examples: The counselor called to set her mind at rest that the housing application had gone through.
collocations: help set your mind at rest

gloss_en: To make someone feel calm and free of worry.

examples: The new library hours will be put into effect at the start of next semester.

gloss_en: To make a plan, rule, or decision start operating.

examples: The biography focuses less on the leader's victories than on the moral character that guided his decisions.

gloss_en: The set of ethical qualities that define a person's principles and behavior.

term: True gold does not fear to be tried by fire.

examples: When critics questioned her results, she stayed calm, trusting that true gold does not fear to be tried by fire.
collocations: True gold does not fear to be tried by fire
as the saying goes, true gold does not fear fire

gloss_en: A proverb meaning that a genuinely good person or thing can withstand any test or hardship.

examples: Exhausted after the field trip, the students sank into slumber the moment the bus started moving.

gloss_en: To fall asleep, especially gradually and deeply.

def_zh: 把某物/某事托付给某人
examples: The professor entrusted the rare specimen to her most careful graduate student.
collocations: entrust the task to a colleague

gloss_en: To give someone the responsibility for taking care of something or someone.

examples: According to the reading, true leadership consists in the ability to inspire others, not in issuing commands.

gloss_en: To have something as its main or essential feature; to be based on.

examples: The lecture described how independent courts are meant to uphold justice even against the government itself.

gloss_en: To support and maintain fairness and the rule of law.

examples: No real experiment is perfectly controlled, but a good design can approximate the ideal quite closely.

gloss_en: To come close to a perfect or ideal standard without fully reaching it.

examples: In her essay she draws on both economic data and personal interviews to support the argument.

gloss_en: To make use of a resource, such as knowledge or experience, for a particular purpose.

examples: The study found that students who are financially well-off are less likely to take on part-time jobs.

gloss_en: To have plenty of money; to be wealthy.

examples: The university plans to channel funding into renewable-energy research over the next five years.
collocations: channel more funding into the program

gloss_en: To direct money toward a particular purpose or activity.

examples: The scholarship covers only a fraction of the total tuition, so most students still need loans.

gloss_en: A small part or portion of something.

examples: The incubator program is designed to bring the innovative minds on campus together with local investors.

gloss_en: People who are highly creative and full of original ideas.

examples: Budget-wise, the project is on track, but time-wise we're falling behind schedule.

gloss_en: A suffix meaning 'with respect to' or 'in terms of' a particular aspect.

def_zh: 利用，开发，发掘（已有的资源、知识等）
examples: The workshop teaches students how to tap into the university's alumni network when looking for internships.

gloss_en: To make use of an existing resource, source of knowledge, or energy.

examples: The poet often walked alone in the forest to commune with nature and gather ideas for his verse.
collocations: a chance to commune with nature
go off to commune with nature

gloss_en: To feel a close, contemplative connection with the natural world.

def_zh: vi. 与……亲密地交谈；n. 公社
examples: He spent a summer on a farming commune where everyone shared the land and the harvest.

gloss_en: As a verb, to communicate closely or intimately; as a noun, a community whose members share property and responsibilities.

examples: After the exam she wanted nothing more than to recline on a lounge chair and close her eyes.

gloss_en: To lie back in a relaxed position on a long, comfortable chair.

term: the seashore with a gleam of moonlight

examples: The essay opens with an image of the seashore with a gleam of moonlight, setting a quiet, reflective mood.
collocations: the seashore with a gleam of moonlight

gloss_en: A beach softly lit by faint moonlight.

examples: The lecture explained how filial piety shaped family duties and social order in traditional Chinese society.

gloss_en: Respect for and devotion to one's parents and elders.

examples: Moving from a small town to the city, she was startled by the faster tempo of life.
collocations: the fast tempo of modern life

gloss_en: The pace or speed at which people live their daily lives.

def_zh: 彻底的、影响广泛的改变
examples: The new dean announced sweeping changes to the curriculum that will affect every department.

gloss_en: Wide-ranging, thorough changes that affect many things.

examples: The philosopher warned against chasing the ephemeral happiness of material things at the expense of lasting fulfillment.

gloss_en: Happiness that lasts only a very short time.

examples: After graduation she took a position at the bank's East Africa branch to work on microfinance projects.

gloss_en: The local office of the bank located in East Africa.

examples: The essay argues that a good employee should raise concerns openly rather than grovel to the boss.

gloss_en: To behave in an excessively humble or submissive way toward someone to gain favor.

examples: The researcher felt stuck in a groove until a colleague suggested a completely new experimental method.
collocations: stuck in the same old groove

gloss_en: To be trapped in a fixed, unchanging routine or way of thinking.

examples: The novel's small village becomes the epitome of the society, mirroring its class divisions in miniature.

gloss_en: A perfect example that captures the essential nature of a whole society.

examples: Historians treat the tax protests as the prelude of this revolution, hinting at the upheaval to come.

gloss_en: The events or period that come immediately before and lead into a revolution.

term: Two in distress makes sorrow less.

examples: The two exchange students bonded over their homesickness, proving that two in distress makes sorrow less.
collocations: Two in distress makes sorrow less

gloss_en: A proverb meaning that sharing trouble with someone in the same situation makes it easier to bear.

def_zh: 碎片, 粉碎, 落叶, 喷洒
examples: The shatter of the dropped vase brought everyone running into the hall.

def_en: break into many pieces
damage or destroy
cause to break into many pieces
def_zh: 打碎, 使散开, 粉碎, 破坏
粉碎, 损坏, 脱落
examples: The stone struck the window and shattered it into a thousand glittering fragments.

gloss_en: to break suddenly into many small pieces, or to destroy something such as hopes or confidence completely.

examples: Choosing to abandon the widely accepted theory was a momentous decision for the young scientist.

gloss_en: A decision of great importance with far-reaching consequences.

examples: The economics lecture described how new security features make counterfeit currency far harder to produce.

gloss_en: Fake money made to imitate genuine currency.

examples: This laboratory became the spearhead of the university's push into artificial-intelligence research.

gloss_en: The person or group leading an attack, campaign, or movement.

examples: Trained observers can discern the subtle changes in a bird's plumage as it matures.

gloss_en: To notice slight differences or shifts that are not obvious.

term: the interplay of fact and fiction

def_zh: 事实与虚构的相互作用
examples: The seminar explores the interplay of fact and fiction in historical novels that dramatize real events.
collocations: the interplay of fact and fiction
the interplay of light and shadow

gloss_en: The way factual and invented elements influence and blend with each other.

examples: The professor argued that smaller seminar classes are more conducive to open discussion than large lectures.

gloss_en: to help make something happen or make it easier; to create favorable conditions for something

examples: The groundskeeper put up signs asking students not to trample on the lawn while the new grass takes root.
collocations: keep off the lawn to avoid trampling it

gloss_en: to walk heavily on the grass and damage it by stepping on it

term: a coupon for a half-off dinner

examples: The campus dining app gave every new student a coupon for a half-off dinner at the student union restaurant.
collocations: a coupon for a free dessert
the coupon expires at the end of the month

gloss_en: a printed or digital voucher that lets you buy a dinner at fifty percent of the normal price

def_zh: 过着诗情画意田园牧歌般的生活
examples: In the lecture, the professor noted that many nineteenth-century painters imagined farmers leading an idyllic life far from the noisy cities.

gloss_en: to live in a peaceful, simple, and happy way, like the charming rural life described in poems

def_en: half asleep
showing lack of attention or boredom
examples: The warm room and soft music made everyone feel drowsy after lunch.

gloss_en: feeling sleepy and not fully alert.

examples: The sociology lecture challenged the deep-rooted notion that intelligence is fixed at birth.

gloss_en: a belief or idea that is firmly fixed in people's minds and very hard to change

examples: The professor traced the notion of social education back to reformers who believed museums and libraries could teach the public.
collocations: embrace the notion of social education
reject a narrow notion of education

gloss_en: the idea or concept that education should take place through society and community life, not only in schools

def_zh: 轰动的/耸人听闻的新闻
examples: The journalism professor warned that a sensational piece of news may attract readers but often sacrifices accuracy.

gloss_en: a news story that causes great excitement or shock, often exaggerated to attract attention

examples: In the debate tournament, our team finally faced a qualified adversary who forced us to sharpen every argument.

gloss_en: a strong, capable opponent who has the skills to compete seriously against you

def_zh: 一份不错的工作（薪水、待遇、环境等）
examples: Many graduating seniors visit the career center hoping a strong internship will help them land a decent job.

gloss_en: a job that is reasonably good in pay, benefits, and working conditions

examples: Failing the first quiz dented her confidence, but the professor assured her it counted for very little of the final grade.
collocations: a serious dent in his confidence
boost rather than dent her confidence

gloss_en: to damage or weaken someone's confidence, making them feel less sure of themselves

examples: The urban studies lecture explained why factories were often built on the periphery of the city, where land was cheaper.
collocations: on the periphery of the city

gloss_en: the outer edge or surrounding areas of a city, away from its center

examples: The professor explained that early colonial assemblies met only a few weeks each year to take care of legislative duties.

gloss_en: to carry out the official responsibilities involved in making laws

examples: The librarian described the university archive as a reservoir of information for anyone studying local history.
collocations: tap into a reservoir of information

gloss_en: a large stored supply of information that can be drawn on, like water kept in a reservoir

term: barge one's way through the crowds

examples: He had to barge his way through the crowds at the bookstore to grab the last used copy of the textbook.
collocations: push one's way through the crowd

gloss_en: to push forward roughly and forcefully through a group of people

examples: The linguistics professor argued that children acquire a foreign language more easily when they are immersed in it daily.

gloss_en: to learn a foreign language until you can use it, especially gradually through exposure and practice

examples: The library is always packed during finals week, so you should reserve a study room in advance.

gloss_en: to be very crowded, completely full of people

examples: Halfway through the two-hour lecture, my concentration lapsed and I missed the key definition on the slide.

gloss_en: someone's attention weakened or drifted away for a period of time

def_zh: 具有道德指导意义的小说
examples: The literature professor described the eighteenth-century novel as a morally instructive fiction meant to guide young readers.

gloss_en: a story or novel written to teach readers moral lessons about right and wrong behavior

examples: The history lecture explained how the crown's decision to levy taxes on the colonists without their consent sparked protests.

gloss_en: to officially impose and collect taxes from someone, using government authority

examples: The seminar discussion revolved around whether social media has changed how students form friendships.
collocations: the plot revolves around a family

gloss_en: to have something as the main subject or central focus; to center on something

examples: According to the lecture, the new university president instituted a series of reforms to modernize the curriculum.
collocations: carry out a series of reforms

gloss_en: to officially introduce and put into effect a set of changes intended to improve a system

examples: Winning the poster competition really bolstered her confidence before the graduate school interviews.

gloss_en: to strengthen or increase someone's confidence and self-belief

examples: The environmental science lecture warned that several coastal wetland species are in grave peril from rising sea levels.

gloss_en: in very serious danger; facing a severe crisis

examples: The dean will preside over the awards ceremony at the end of the semester.
collocations: preside over a period of change

gloss_en: to be in charge of and lead a meeting, organization, or event; to have authority over something

examples: The professor explained how the treaty brought the decade-long conflict to an end.
collocations: bring the war to an end
bring the discussion to an end

gloss_en: to make something finish or stop; to conclude something

examples: The archaeology lecture showed a clay tablet inscribed with records of grain trades from four thousand years ago.
collocations: a monument inscribed with a dedication

gloss_en: to have words or symbols carved or written on a surface

examples: The professor noted that the railroad allowed farmers in outlying areas to ship produce to city markets for the first time.

gloss_en: places that are far from the center of a city or region; remote districts

examples: The student judicial board is presided over by a senior elected each spring.
collocations: a meeting presided over by the chair
a court presided over by a judge
a ceremony presided over by the dean

gloss_en: to be led, managed, or controlled by a particular person in authority

examples: In the lecture on ancient Athens, the professor explained that attending the assembly was among the most important civic duties.

gloss_en: the responsibilities that citizens are expected to fulfill toward their community and government, such as voting or jury service

term: a breathtaking view of the mountains

examples: From the observatory on the hill, visitors get a breathtaking view of the mountains to the north of campus.
collocations: a breathtaking view of the coastline

gloss_en: an extremely beautiful and impressive sight of the mountains

examples: The professor said the mathematical proof lies beyond the scope of this introductory course, but interested students can read the appendix.
collocations: beyond the scope of this paper
within the scope of the study

gloss_en: to be outside the range of what a discussion, study, or course is intended to cover

examples: The new fossil evidence casts doubt on the earlier theory about when the species migrated across the land bridge.
collocations: cast serious doubt on the findings

gloss_en: to make people feel less certain that something is true or reliable

examples: The professor pointed out that even a failed experiment serves a useful purpose by ruling out one explanation.

gloss_en: to be genuinely useful; to perform a helpful function

examples: In her thesis defense, she invoked eminent scholars in the field to justify her choice of methodology.

gloss_en: to cite or refer to famous, highly respected scholars in order to support one's argument

examples: The biography lecture described how the inventor rose from humble origins to head one of the country's largest laboratories.

gloss_en: to start life in a poor or low social position and become successful

examples: The debate coach warned us that the visiting team would be a formidable opponent this year.

gloss_en: an opponent who is very strong and difficult to defeat, inspiring respect or fear

def_en: a wilderness at the edge of a settled area of a country
an international boundary or the area (often fortified) immediately inside the boundary
an undeveloped field of study; a topic inviting research and development
examples: The settlers pushed westward, building homes on the frontier of the new territory.

def_zh: 国境, 边境, 边界

gloss_en: the border between two countries, or the far edge of a settled region beyond which land is still wild.

term: push back the frontiers of science

examples: The guest speaker said that well-funded university labs are essential if we want to push back the frontiers of science.
collocations: push back the frontiers of knowledge

gloss_en: to extend the limits of scientific knowledge by exploring new areas of research

examples: The advisor said I would be ill-advised to take five lab courses in the same semester.

gloss_en: to be unwise or imprudent in doing something; doing it shows poor judgment

examples: After one introductory astronomy class, she developed a fascination with black holes and switched her major to physics.

gloss_en: to gradually form a strong interest in and attraction to something

examples: The professor explained how paper-making techniques made their way to Europe along the trade routes.
collocations: make one's way to the exit
make one's way through the crowd
make one's way in the world

gloss_en: to travel or move toward a place, often gradually or with effort; (of things) to eventually reach a place

examples: The lecture argued that by the 1920s, radio had eclipsed newspapers in importance as a source of breaking news.
collocations: be eclipsed by a newer technology

gloss_en: A becomes more important than B, making B seem less significant by comparison

def_zh: 高度分化分阶层的社会
examples: The anthropology professor described ancient Egypt as a highly stratified society, with priests and officials far above farmers.

gloss_en: a society that is sharply divided into distinct social classes or layers with unequal status

examples: The resident advisor will see to it that every freshman gets a copy of the dorm regulations.
collocations: see to it that everything is ready

gloss_en: to make sure that something happens; to take responsibility for ensuring a result

examples: The professor noted that the composer died prematurely at thirty-five, leaving his final symphony unfinished.

gloss_en: to die earlier than expected or before reaching old age

examples: The advisor cautioned that declaring a major in your first semester can be a premature decision.

gloss_en: a decision made too early, before enough thought or information is available; a hasty decision

examples: The lecture described writers of the period who rejected city life and moved to small farming communities.
collocations: turn one's back on city life

gloss_en: to refuse or turn away from living in a city because one dislikes it

term: a partial solution to the problem

def_zh: 对问题的部分/不完全解决
examples: The professor admitted that recycling is only a partial solution to the problem of plastic waste.
collocations: a complete solution to the problem

gloss_en: a solution that solves only part of a problem, not all of it

examples: The lecturer explained how advances in medical science doubled average life expectancy over the past century.

gloss_en: the branch of science concerned with treating illness and maintaining health; medicine as a field of study

examples: The lab demonstration went smoothly, so we finished the experiment twenty minutes early.

gloss_en: to proceed without problems or interruptions

examples: The campus bookstore finally started selling used textbooks at a reasonable price.

gloss_en: a price that is fair and not too high; an acceptable amount to pay

examples: The botany lecture explained that volcanic ash eventually breaks down into hospitable soil for native shrubs.

gloss_en: soil that provides good conditions for plants to grow in

examples: Early settlers were drawn to the valley by its hospitable climate and reliable rainfall.

gloss_en: a climate that is pleasant and favorable for living or growing things

def_zh: 很少有文件记载的原始资料
examples: The historian admitted that the folk songs are primary material with little documentation, so dating them precisely is difficult.

gloss_en: original, first-hand source material that has few written records to explain or verify it

examples: The campus doctor warned him that pulling all-nighters would only aggravate his illness.

gloss_en: to make someone's illness worse or more serious

examples: By 1900, the professor noted, the United States had surpassed Britain in steel production.

gloss_en: A does better than B or exceeds B in a particular quality or area

examples: The admissions officer said the essay should demonstrate genuine intellectual curiosity, not just a list of achievements.

gloss_en: a strong desire to learn and understand new ideas and knowledge

examples: The health center nurse reminded students never to exceed the recommended dosage of over-the-counter painkillers during exam stress.

gloss_en: to take more of a medicine than the amount officially advised

term: a holistic solution to the problem

def_zh: 对问题的整体性/全面的解决
examples: The professor argued that reducing traffic requires a holistic solution to the problem, combining transit, zoning, and pricing.

gloss_en: a solution that deals with the whole problem and all its connected parts, rather than just one aspect

examples: The economics lecture used 1920s Germany as a classic case of rampant inflation destroying household savings.

gloss_en: inflation that is rising quickly and out of control

def_zh: 不断蔓延的/肆虐的疾病
examples: The professor explained that rampant disease in the crowded port cities forced officials to build the first public sewers.

gloss_en: a disease that is spreading widely and uncontrollably

examples: The discovery of water ice on the asteroid was explosive news in the planetary science community.

gloss_en: shocking, dramatic news that causes a strong public reaction

examples: The history professor described the weeks before the strike as an explosive situation that the mayor failed to defuse.

gloss_en: a tense, dangerous situation that could suddenly become violent or get out of control

def_en: a small cosmetics case with a mirror; to be carried in a woman's purse
a small and economical car
examples: She opened a small compact to check her make-up in its little mirror.

def_en: have the property of being packable or of compacting easily
examples: The machine compacts the loose rubbish into dense blocks that are easier to transport.

def_en: closely and firmly united or packed together
def_zh: 紧凑的, 紧密的, 简洁的
examples: The new laptop is remarkably compact, fitting easily into even a small handbag.

gloss_en: closely and neatly packed together, taking up little space.

examples: In tonight's lab, we'll use the telescope to track a celestial object as it moves across the sky.

gloss_en: a natural body in space, such as a star, planet, moon, or comet

examples: The dean responded to the reporters with a carefully phrased remark that avoided blaming anyone directly.

gloss_en: a comment whose wording has been chosen with great care and precision

examples: The geology lecture explained how the Great Lakes came into existence as glaciers retreated at the end of the Ice Age.

gloss_en: to begin to exist; to be created or formed

examples: The lecture on the 1930s strikes described how the governor deployed security forces to keep the factories open.

gloss_en: to position and put security troops or police into action in a particular place

examples: The professor was loath to extend the deadline, but the campus-wide power outage left her no choice.

gloss_en: to be unwilling or reluctant to do something

examples: The lecture pointed out that in the early republic, only a small fraction of adults were eligible to vote.

gloss_en: to meet the official requirements that allow a person to vote

examples: The scholarship is reserved for financially needy students who maintain a strong GPA.

gloss_en: lacking enough money; in financial hardship and needing support

examples: After the campus fun run, volunteers handed out bottled water so runners could quench their thirst.

gloss_en: to drink something so that you stop feeling thirsty

examples: The professor explained that annual floods replenish nutrients in the soil, which is why early farmers settled along the river.

gloss_en: to restore or refill nutrients that have been used up

term: the optimal time for doing sth

examples: According to the biology lecture, early morning is the optimal time for observing songbird activity.

gloss_en: the best or most favorable time to do something

examples: The student council holds an open forum each month where residents can air their grievances about housing.

gloss_en: to express complaints or feelings of unfair treatment openly, so others can hear them

examples: The novel's main character nurses a grievance against his former business partner for decades.

gloss_en: to keep feeling resentment toward someone for a long time because you believe they treated you unfairly

examples: The advisor said the exchange program offers ample opportunities to practice the language with native speakers.

gloss_en: to provide plenty of chances to do something

examples: The geography lecture contrasted the sparse population of the high plains with the dense population of the coastal cities.

gloss_en: a population that is thinly spread over an area (sparse) or crowded closely together (dense)

examples: During my first class presentation I stumbled over my words, but the professor's feedback helped me improve.

gloss_en: to speak in a hesitant, awkward way, making mistakes or pausing because of nervousness

examples: She stumbled and fell on the icy steps outside the dorm, so she went to the health center to check her ankle.

gloss_en: to trip while walking and fall to the ground

examples: In its heyday, the professor explained, the canal carried more freight than any road in the region.
collocations: in the heyday of rail travel

gloss_en: during the period when a person or thing was most successful, popular, or powerful

examples: Check the bulletin board outside the department office — the schedule for review sessions was posted this morning.
collocations: post a notice on the bulletin board
pin a flyer to the board

gloss_en: a board, physical or online, where notices and announcements are posted for people to read

examples: The lecture described the horrible plight of farm families during the Dust Bowl, when crops failed year after year.

gloss_en: to be in a very bad, difficult, or distressing situation

examples: In the optics demonstration, the professor compared how light behaves with a clear beaker and an opaque glass.

gloss_en: a glass that light cannot pass through, so you cannot see what is inside it

examples: The window was fitted with an opaque, a frosted panel that let in light but hid the room.

def_en: not transmitting or reflecting light or radiant energy; impenetrable to sight
not clearly understood or expressed
def_zh: 不透明的, 不传热的, 不传导的, 阴暗的
examples: The bathroom window is made of opaque glass so no one can see inside.

gloss_en: not able to be seen through; not transparent. Also, hard to understand.

examples: When the snowstorm canceled all flights, dozens of students were stranded at the airport over winter break.

gloss_en: to be stuck in a place and unable to leave, usually because transportation is unavailable

examples: After trying three different majors, he finally found his niche in environmental engineering.

gloss_en: to find the role, job, or area of activity that suits you perfectly

term: spot a niche in the market

examples: The business lecture described how the founders spotted a niche in the market for affordable meal kits aimed at students.
collocations: fill a niche in the market
identify a gap in the market

gloss_en: to notice a business opportunity for a product or service that no one else is providing

def_zh: 针对某群体的产品营销
examples: In the marketing seminar, we analyzed how niche marketing helped a tiny outdoor-gear company survive against big retailers.

gloss_en: the practice of aiming a product or service at a small, specific group of customers

def_en: a compartment in a stable where a single animal is confined and fed
a booth where articles are displayed for sale
a malfunction in the flight of an aircraft in which there is a sudden loss of lift that results in a downward plunge
seating in the forward part of the main level of a theater
def_zh: 厩, 停车处, 牧师职位, 货摊, 托辞, 拖延
examples: She bought fresh strawberries from a fruit stall in the middle of the market square.

def_zh: 关入厩, 停顿, 推托, 支吾, 使陷于泥中
被关在厩内, 陷于泥中, 停止, 支吾
examples: The negotiations stalled when neither side was willing to make the first concession.

gloss_en: a small stand or table from which goods are sold, often at a market; also to stop making progress or delay.

examples: When the professor called on him, he stalled for time by rereading the question aloud while gathering his thoughts.

gloss_en: to deliberately delay in order to gain more time before acting or answering

examples: The case study showed the supplier stalling on the deal until market prices moved in its favor.
collocations: drag one's feet on an agreement

gloss_en: to deliberately delay reaching or completing an agreement

term: deposit valuables in the hotel safe

def_zh: 把贵重物品寄存在酒店保险柜
examples: Before the conference sessions began, our advisor told us to deposit our valuables in the hotel safe.

gloss_en: to place valuable belongings in the hotel's secure storage box for safekeeping

def_en: an idea that preoccupies the mind and holds the attention
the mental state of being preoccupied by something
def_zh: 抢先占据, 先人之见, 入神
examples: His constant preoccupation with money left him little time to enjoy life.

def_zh: 成见, 注意散漫, 心不在焉

gloss_en: the state of thinking or worrying about something so much that you pay little attention to other things.

examples: The economics professor explained how public works programs were designed to bolster up the economy during the Depression.

gloss_en: to support and strengthen the economy, helping it improve

examples: In the lecture, the professor explains how governments cut taxes during a recession to stimulate the economy.

gloss_en: to encourage economic activity and growth, for example through government spending or lower interest rates

examples: Several students raised an objection to the university's plan to shorten library hours during exam week.

gloss_en: to formally express disagreement with or opposition to a plan, idea, or proposal

examples: The history professor notes that the small scale peasant economy dominated rural China before industrialization began.
collocations: a traditional small scale peasant economy
the decline of the peasant economy

gloss_en: an economic system based on individual farming families who cultivate small plots of land mainly for their own subsistence

examples: The professor explains that canning dramatically extended the shelf life of vegetables, transforming the food industry.
collocations: have a shelf life of six months

gloss_en: the length of time a product, especially food, remains usable or safe to consume before it spoils

examples: The environmental science department initiated a project to monitor water quality in the campus lake.

gloss_en: to formally start or launch a project or program of work

examples: As a rule of thumb, the advisor says you should spend two hours studying for every hour of lecture.
collocations: follow a simple rule of thumb

gloss_en: a rough practical principle based on experience rather than exact calculation

examples: The professor asks the class how the theory of plate tectonics relates to the formation of volcanic islands.

gloss_en: A is connected with or has to do with B

examples: The seminar room was changed at short notice, so several students went to the wrong building.

gloss_en: with very little advance warning; almost immediately after being told

examples: The ecology team ran an in-situ experiment on the reef to see how corals respond to warmer water in their natural habitat.

gloss_en: an experiment carried out in the actual place or natural setting being studied, rather than in a laboratory

examples: The anthropology lecturer points out that cutting hide with a blunt knife would have forced early humans to develop sharpening techniques.

gloss_en: a knife whose edge is dull and does not cut well

examples: The speaker argues that unchecked strip mining can demolish the environment of an entire valley within a decade.

gloss_en: to severely damage or destroy the natural environment

examples: The professor asks each student to write one paragraph that encapsulates the central themes of the novel.

gloss_en: to summarize or express the main ideas of something in a brief, concentrated form

examples: In the story discussed in class, the narrator is portrayed as a sophisticated young man who feels out of place in his rural hometown.

gloss_en: a young man who is worldly, cultured, and experienced beyond his years

def_zh: 十分先进的计算机系统
examples: The lecturer explains that modern weather forecasting relies on highly sophisticated computer systems running millions of calculations per second.

gloss_en: computer systems that are extremely advanced and complex in design and capability

examples: The literature professor argues that the poem captures a fleeting moment of happiness before the speaker returns to grief.

gloss_en: a moment of happiness that lasts only a very short time before disappearing

def_en: one who stays for only a short time
(physics) a short-lived oscillation in a system caused by a sudden change of voltage or current or load
def_zh: 瞬, 短期居留者, 过境鸟, 瞬变现象
examples: The cheap hotel near the station was mostly used by transients passing through the city.

def_zh: 短暂的, 易变的, 瞬变的, 路过的
examples: Fame can be transient, disappearing as quickly as it arrives.

def_zh: 瞬变过程; 过渡过程; 过渡的; 瞬变的; 瞬时峰值电压

gloss_en: lasting only a short time; (as a noun) a person who stays in a place for only a brief period.

examples: The registrar's office will furnish you with an official transcript within three business days.

gloss_en: to supply or provide someone with something they need, such as information or equipment

examples: The professor alluded to next week's exam several times without saying exactly what it would cover.

gloss_en: to mention something indirectly or hint at it without stating it openly

examples: The economics lecture describes how hoarding and profiteering during wartime shortages forced governments to introduce price controls.
collocations: crack down on hoarding and profiteering

gloss_en: buying up and storing scarce goods in order to sell them later at unfairly high prices

examples: The career counselor reminds students that arriving early to an interview helps make a favorable impression on the employer.
collocations: make a favorable impression on the interviewer

gloss_en: to cause someone to form a positive opinion of you

examples: The biology professor describes how squirrels forage for nuts in autumn and bury them for the winter.

gloss_en: to search widely for food or supplies, such as firewood or clothing, especially in the wild

examples: The zoology lecturer notes that wolves cover enormous distances with a loping stride that conserves energy.

gloss_en: a long, relaxed, bounding step used when moving at an easy pace

examples: As the paramount of the region, the old chief settled every serious dispute among the villages.

def_zh: 最重要的, 最高的, 至上的
examples: For the airline, passenger safety is of paramount importance during every flight.

def_en: s having superior power and influence

gloss_en: more important than anything else; supreme in rank, power, or importance.

def_en: a crosspiece spreading the gunnels of a boat; used as a seat in a rowboat
examples: The rower sat on the wooden thwart that stretched across the middle of the boat.

def_en: hinder or prevent (the efforts, plans, or desires) of
def_zh: 反对, 阻挠, 横过
examples: Quick action by the guards thwarted the prisoners' attempt to escape.
A thwart beam ran across the frame to hold the two sides together.
The fallen log lay thwart across the narrow forest path, blocking the way.
A sudden gust drove the small boat thwart the current toward the rocks.

gloss_en: to prevent someone from doing something or stop a plan from succeeding

def_en: land received by fee tail
the act of entailing property; the creation of a fee tail from a fee simple
examples: Under the old entail, the family estate could pass only to the eldest son.

def_en: have as a logical consequence
impose, involve, or imply as a necessary accompaniment or result
def_zh: 使成为必需, 需要, 使承担, 遗传给
examples: Running a small business entails long hours and a great deal of financial risk.

gloss_en: to involve something as a necessary part or consequence

def_en: a means to an end; not necessarily a principled or ethical one
examples: Borrowing from friends was a temporary expedient until his salary arrived.

def_en: serving to promote your interest
appropriate to a purpose; practical
def_zh: 权宜的, 方便的, 有用的
examples: The government found it politically expedient to postpone the unpopular decision.

gloss_en: a method or action that is useful for achieving a particular purpose, even if it is not fully fair or moral; also, convenient and practical for a purpose.

examples: After one semester of the tutoring program, the study reported measurable improvements in students' writing scores.

gloss_en: improvements that are large or clear enough to be observed and quantified

examples: The dean explains that the redesigned campus website is meant to present a modern image to prospective students.
collocations: present a modern image to the public

gloss_en: to deliberately show oneself or an organization as up-to-date and contemporary

def_en: worth having or seeking or achieving
worthy of being chosen especially as a spouse
def_zh: 令人想望的, 可取的
examples: Strong communication skills are highly desirable in almost every profession today.

gloss_en: worth having or wanting because it is useful, attractive, or of good quality.

def_zh: 损害/侵犯某人的隐私(权)
examples: Some students argue that installing cameras in dormitory hallways would infringe upon their privacy.

gloss_en: to intrude on or violate someone's right to keep their personal life private

examples: The committee approved the new course in principle, but the syllabus still needs final review.
collocations: in principle but not in practice

gloss_en: in general terms or in theory, even if the details or practice may differ

examples: The urban studies lecture examines how a new light-rail line helped revitalize the economy of the city's downtown district.
collocations: a plan to revitalize the economy

gloss_en: to bring new strength and activity back to an economy that has weakened

examples: The sociology professor cites surveys showing that many part-time students struggle to juggle work with family commitments.

gloss_en: to try to manage the competing demands of a job and family responsibilities at the same time

term: make a passing mention of sth

examples: The textbook makes only a passing mention of the experiment, so the professor spends a full lecture on it.
collocations: make a passing mention of the issue

gloss_en: to refer to something only briefly and without going into detail

examples: In the interest of time, the professor asked students to hold their questions until the end of the lecture.

gloss_en: for the purpose of achieving or protecting something, such as fairness, safety, or time

examples: The student council candidate won the election by a large margin, taking nearly seventy percent of the vote.
collocations: exceed expectations by a large margin

gloss_en: by a big difference in amount, votes, or degree

def_zh: 与……建立密切和谐的关系
examples: The teaching assistant quickly established a rapport with the freshmen, which made discussion sections much livelier.
collocations: a good rapport between teacher and class

gloss_en: to build a friendly, harmonious relationship marked by mutual understanding and trust

examples: The advisor's friendly tone put the anxious first-year student at ease during their first meeting.

gloss_en: to make someone feel relaxed and comfortable instead of nervous

examples: The lecturer stresses that the migration patterns of these birds are intimately tied to seasonal changes in food supply.
collocations: be intimately tied to the environment

gloss_en: to be very closely connected with or dependent on something

def_en: driving or forcing
tending to persuade by forcefulness of argument
def_zh: 强制的, 强迫性的, 激发兴趣的
examples: The lawyer presented a compelling argument that left the jury with little room for doubt.

gloss_en: so interesting, powerful, or convincing that it holds your attention or forces you to agree.

term: engage both the mind and the eye

examples: The art history professor praises the exhibition for engaging both the mind and the eye with its interactive displays.

gloss_en: to be both intellectually interesting and visually attractive

examples: She was so caught up in her lab work that she completely forgot about the department meeting.
collocations: be caught up in the excitement
be caught up in a dispute

gloss_en: to be so involved in or trapped by a situation that it absorbs or controls you

examples: On the surface, the two poems seem similar, but the professor shows that their underlying themes are completely different.
collocations: on the surface, it appears that

gloss_en: judging only by outward appearance, which may hide a different underlying reality

term: gauge the saliency of an issue

def_zh: 衡量一个问题的显著性
examples: The political science lecture explains how pollsters gauge the saliency of an issue before an election by asking voters what concerns them most.

gloss_en: to measure or judge how prominent and important an issue is to people

examples: The professor said that quantum field theory is beyond the scope of this introductory course.
collocations: beyond the scope of this paper/course

gloss_en: outside the range of topics that a discussion, study, or course is intended to cover

examples: The professor argues that the composer's late works are imbued with a deep sense of nostalgia.
collocations: be imbued with a sense of

gloss_en: to be filled or deeply penetrated with a quality, feeling, or idea

examples: The new librarian is well versed in digital archives, so she can help you locate historical newspapers online.
collocations: be well versed in the literature
well versed in the art of

gloss_en: to be very knowledgeable about or skilled in a subject

examples: The history lecture examines how medieval guilds wielded power over trade in European cities.

gloss_en: to hold and exercise power or authority, especially great power

examples: The urban planning professor attributes the town's economic vibrancy to the steady flow of university students and visitors.
collocations: the economic vibrancy of a city

gloss_en: the lively, flourishing condition of an economy with active business and growth

def_zh: 把责任不偏不倚地归咎于
examples: The report places the blame squarely on poor maintenance for the laboratory equipment failure.
collocations: place the blame squarely on someone's shoulders

gloss_en: to say directly and unambiguously that a particular person or thing is responsible for something bad

examples: Archaeologists work slowly at the excavation site to preserve the integrity of the buried structures.
collocations: preserve the integrity of the data

gloss_en: to keep something whole, undamaged, and in its original sound condition

examples: The history professor describes how the colonial government censored newspapers it suspected of subversive activities.

gloss_en: actions intended to undermine or overthrow an established system, government, or institution

examples: My advisor speaks highly of that internship program, so I decided to apply this summer.

gloss_en: to praise someone or something; to express a very good opinion of them

term: carry out the responsibility of doing sth

examples: As lab monitor, he carries out the responsibility of checking that all equipment is shut down each evening.

gloss_en: to fulfill or perform a duty that one is obliged to do

examples: In the novel we discussed, the judge is presented as a morally upright man who refuses every bribe.

gloss_en: a man who is honest and behaves according to strong ethical principles

examples: Needless to say, students who missed the review session found the midterm much harder.

gloss_en: used to introduce something so obvious that it hardly needs to be stated

examples: The professor argues that citing sources without reading them violates the principle of academic honesty.

gloss_en: to act against or break an established rule or fundamental standard

def_zh: 抄近道；偷工减料；以简便方法做事
examples: The professor warned us not to cut corners on the data analysis, even though the deadline is close.

gloss_en: to do something in the quickest or cheapest way by skipping steps, often lowering quality

def_en: lack of attention and due care
willful lack of care and attention
examples: The driver showed a complete disregard for the safety of other road users.
Please disregard the previous email, as the meeting time has now changed.

gloss_en: to ignore something or treat it as unimportant; also, a lack of proper attention or respect.

examples: The lecture argues that some cities pursued rapid growth at the expense of air quality.

gloss_en: achieved by sacrificing or harming something else

term: be well on one's way to

examples: Having finished all her required courses, she is well on her way to completing the degree a semester early.
collocations: be well on one's way to success
be well on the way to recovery

gloss_en: to have made steady progress and be likely to achieve something soon

examples: Halfway through the three-hour seminar, his rumbling stomach reminded him he had skipped lunch.

gloss_en: a stomach making low growling noises, usually because of hunger

examples: The psychology lecture links time scarcity to poorer decision-making among overworked students.

gloss_en: the condition of not having enough time to do everything one needs or wants to do

def_zh: 按照你认为合适的方式
examples: The professor said we may organize the group presentation as we see fit, as long as everyone speaks.
collocations: use the funds as you see fit

gloss_en: in whatever way you personally judge to be appropriate or suitable

examples: The new recreation center is operating under provisional rules until the student committee approves a final policy.

gloss_en: temporary rules that apply until permanent ones are established

examples: In the law lecture, the professor contrasts a civil case, where one party sues another for damages, with a criminal trial.

gloss_en: a legal dispute between private parties over rights or money, as opposed to a criminal prosecution

examples: If I were in your shoes, I would talk to the professor before dropping the course.
collocations: if I were in your shoes

gloss_en: to be in the same situation as someone else, especially a difficult one

examples: The commencement speaker urged graduates to stop waiting for the perfect moment and set their lives in motion.

gloss_en: to take action that starts one's life moving toward its goals or a new direction

examples: Facing a budget crisis, the university took drastic measures, including freezing all new hiring.
collocations: take drastic measures to cut costs

gloss_en: to take extreme, forceful steps to deal with a serious problem

examples: "Okay, let's switch gears," the professor said, "and move from photosynthesis to cellular respiration."

gloss_en: to suddenly change the topic or shift to a different activity or approach

examples: You can retake the placement test, provided that you register at least a week in advance.

gloss_en: on the condition that; only if a stated requirement is met

def_en: trace the shape of
describe in vivid detail
def_zh: 描绘...的轮廓, 描绘, 描写
examples: The report clearly delineates the responsibilities of each team member.

gloss_en: to describe or mark out something precisely, especially its limits or main features.

examples: The testing center schedules extra time to accommodate the special needs of students with documented disabilities.

gloss_en: to adjust arrangements so as to meet the particular requirements of certain people

examples: The lecturer explains that the architect drew inspiration from termite mounds when designing the building's ventilation system.

gloss_en: to get creative ideas or motivation from a particular source

term: not so much A as B

examples: The professor argues that the city's decline was not so much an economic failure as a failure of planning.
collocations: not so much a problem as an opportunity
it is not so much X as Y

gloss_en: used to say that B is a more accurate description than A

examples: The sociology lecture explains how sensational news coverage can feed into public anxiety about crime.

gloss_en: to contribute to and intensify a situation, feeling, or process

examples: Critics say some economists live in an ivory tower, building models that ignore how real consumers behave.

gloss_en: a situation, especially academic life, that is disconnected from the practical realities of ordinary people

examples: The professor notes that regulators took drastic action after the water samples showed dangerous contamination levels.

gloss_en: to act in an extreme and forceful way to solve a serious problem

examples: The business lecture examines why the factory laid off two hundred workers when demand for its products collapsed.

gloss_en: to dismiss employees from their jobs, usually because the employer needs to cut costs

def_en: characterized by friendship and good will
examples: The two companies reached an amicable agreement without going to court.

def_zh: 友好的, 和睦的, 温和的

gloss_en: friendly and without serious disagreement or bad feeling.

examples: The history professor describes the treaty as an attempt to restore amity between the two former rivals.

gloss_en: friendly and peaceful relations, especially between groups or nations

examples: The literature seminar focuses on the poet's amatory verses, written for a woman he never named.

gloss_en: relating to romantic or sexual love; typical of lovers

def_en: disposed to please
def_zh: 亲切的, 和蔼可亲的
examples: The new manager was so amiable that everyone in the office felt at ease with her.

def_zh: 亲和的, 和蔼可亲的

gloss_en: having a friendly, pleasant, and good-natured manner.

def_en: disposed or willing to comply
readily reacting to suggestions and influences
open to being acted upon in a certain way
def_zh: 服从的, 会接纳的, 有义务的
examples: The manager was amenable to our proposal, provided we could finish the project within budget.

def_zh: 服从的, 有责任的, 有服从义务的

gloss_en: willing to accept or agree to something; easily persuaded or open to suggestion.

examples: In the primatology lecture, the professor compares the skull structure of anthropoid apes with that of early humans.

gloss_en: resembling a human being; also, an ape of the group that includes gorillas and chimpanzees

examples: The biology professor explains that the orangutan spends most of its life alone in the forest canopy.

gloss_en: a large red-haired great ape that lives in the rainforests of Borneo and Sumatra

def_en: large terrestrial monkeys having doglike muzzles
examples: A troop of baboons scavenged for food along the edge of the campsite.

gloss_en: a large African or Asian monkey with a long, dog-like snout and a short tail.

def_en: intelligent somewhat arboreal ape of equatorial African forests
examples: Researchers watched a chimpanzee use a stick to pull insects out of a hole in the tree.

gloss_en: a small, intelligent African ape that is closely related to humans and lives mainly in forests

examples: The lecture traces how nineteenth-century industrialists turned to philanthropy, funding libraries and universities across the country.

gloss_en: the practice of giving money or help to promote the welfare of others; organized charity

def_en: inhabiting or frequenting trees
resembling a tree in form and branching structure
examples: Monkeys are highly arboreal animals, spending most of their lives in the forest canopy.

gloss_en: living in or among trees.

examples: The university hired an arborist to assess whether the old oaks near the library could survive the construction work.

gloss_en: a specialist in the care and cultivation of trees

def_en: enlarge or increase
grow or intensify
examples: She took a second job to augment her modest income while paying off her student loans.

def_zh: 增加; 增量; 添加

gloss_en: to increase the size, value, or amount of something.

def_en: commence officially
open ceremoniously or dedicate formally
be a precursor of
def_zh: 开始, 举行...就职典礼
examples: The mayor arrived to inaugurate the new library with a short speech.

gloss_en: to formally begin something important, or to introduce a new official into their position with a ceremony.

def_en: a preceding occurrence or cause or event
anything that precedes something similar in time
the referent of an anaphor; a phrase or clause that is referred to by an anaphoric pronoun
examples: Historians traced the antecedents of the revolution back several decades.
The report ignored the antecedent conditions that had caused the crisis.

gloss_en: a thing or event that comes before another and often leads to it.

examples: In the anatomy lecture, the professor points out that the anterior fins of the fish evolved into forelimbs.

gloss_en: located at or toward the front of the body or a structure

examples: The professor explains that the posterior region of the brain processes visual information.

gloss_en: located at or toward the back of the body or a structure

examples: The robotics lecture discusses why an android that looks almost, but not quite, human can make people uncomfortable.

gloss_en: a robot designed to look and act like a human being

def_zh: 意见一致的, 无异议的
examples: The committee reached a unanimous decision to approve the new proposal.

def_en: s in complete agreement
s acting together as a single undiversified whole

gloss_en: agreed on by everyone in a group; with no one disagreeing.

examples: The philosophy professor asks whether early cultures distinguished animate beings from inanimate objects in their languages.

gloss_en: to give life or energy to something; as an adjective, alive or living

def_en: uncertain or unable to decide about what course to follow
def_zh: 矛盾的, 模棱两可的
examples: She felt ambivalent about the job offer, attracted by the salary but worried about the long hours.

gloss_en: having mixed or contradictory feelings about someone or something, so that you cannot decide clearly.

def_en: collect or gather
examples: Dust had accumulated on the shelves during the months the house stood empty.

gloss_en: to gradually gather or increase in amount over time.

def_en: eager to acquire and possess things especially material possessions or ideas
def_zh: 渴望获得的, 贪婪的
examples: In an increasingly acquisitive society, people often measure success by the number of things they own.

def_zh: 取得的, 获得的, 贪得无厌的

gloss_en: very eager to acquire and own things, especially money or possessions.

def_en: a word formed from the initial letters of the several words in the name
def_zh: 首字母缩拼词, 首字母组合词
examples: "NASA" is an acronym for the National Aeronautics and Space Administration.

gloss_en: a word formed from the first letters of the words in a name or phrase

def_en: an athlete who performs acts requiring skill and agility and coordination
def_zh: 表演特技者, 杂技演员
examples: The acrobat swung across the tent and caught his partner's hands high above the crowd.

gloss_en: a performer who is skilled at difficult physical acts such as balancing, tumbling, and jumping

def_en: a tank or pool or bowl filled with water for keeping live fish and underwater animals
def_zh: 水族馆, 养鱼池, 水草缸

gloss_en: a glass tank filled with water in which fish and other water animals are kept; also a building where such animals are displayed.

def_en: a word that expresses a meaning opposed to the meaning of another word, in which case the two words are antonyms of each other
examples: The word "generous" is an antonym of "stingy," since the two carry opposite meanings.

gloss_en: a word that means the opposite of another word.

examples: The microbiology lecture describes how certain frogs secrete antifungal compounds through their skin.

gloss_en: acting to destroy fungi or prevent their growth

def_en: not dangerous to health; not recurrent or progressive (especially of a tumor)
pleasant and beneficial in nature or influence
kindness of disposition or manner
examples: The doctor reassured her that the tumor was benign and required no further treatment.

gloss_en: gentle and kind; in medicine, describing a growth that is not dangerous to health.

examples: The professor contrasts the king's public image of benevolence with the harsh taxes he imposed on farmers.

gloss_en: kindness and generosity; the desire to do good to others

def_en: financial assistance in time of need
something that aids or promotes well-being
a performance to raise money for a charitable cause
examples: The new policy offers significant financial benefits to low-income families.

def_en: be beneficial for
examples: Students will greatly benefit from the opportunity to study abroad.

gloss_en: An advantage or profit gained from something.

examples: The editor asked the author to excise two chapters that strayed from the book's main argument.
collocations: excise a passage from the text

gloss_en: to cut out or remove something, especially surgically or from a text

def_en: reach, make, or come to a decision about something
bring to an end; settle conclusively
cause to decide
influence or determine

collocations: decide on a course of action

gloss_en: To make a choice or judgment about something, especially after thinking carefully.

def_en: determining or having the power to determine an outcome
unmistakable
characterized by decision and firmness

gloss_en: able to make choices quickly and firmly; or having a strong effect on the final result.

def_en: make an incision into by carving or cutting
examples: The surgeon carefully incised the skin before removing the small tumour.

gloss_en: to cut into a surface, or to carve words or designs into something with a sharp tool

def_en: the killing of a human being by another human being

gloss_en: the crime of killing one person by another.

examples: The environmental science lecture explains how the insecticide DDT accumulated in birds and thinned their eggshells.

gloss_en: a chemical substance used to kill insects

def_en: (of plants and shrubs) shedding foliage at the end of the growing season

def_zh: 每年落叶的, 非永久性的
examples: Oak and maple are deciduous trees that shed their leaves in autumn.

gloss_en: (of a tree or plant) losing its leaves every year at the end of the growing season.

examples: The court found the company culpable for ignoring the safety warnings that led to the accident.

def_en: s deserving blame or censure as being wrong or evil or injurious

gloss_en: the person guilty of a crime or wrongdoing; also, the cause of a problem

def_en: a substance from which another substance is formed (especially by a metabolic reaction)
a person who goes before or announces the coming of another

def_zh: 前体; 前身; 产物母体

examples: The instructor told us to place the cursor at the end of the formula before pressing enter.

gloss_en: the movable indicator on a computer screen that shows where the next action will take place

def_en: a journey taken for pleasure
def_zh: 远足, 游览圆, 短程旅行

gloss_en: moving from topic to topic without a clear plan; rambling and digressive

def_en: the 100th anniversary (or the celebration of it)

def_en: of or relating to or completing a period of 100 years

gloss_en: the 100th anniversary of an important event, or relating to such an anniversary

def_en: a period of 100 years
examples: The city has changed a lot over the past century.

gloss_en: a period of 100 years

def_en: the act of playing a percussion instrument
the act of exploding a percussion cap
tapping a part of the body for diagnostic purposes
def_zh: 敲打, 碰撞, 叩击, 叩诊, 击发, 打击, 打击乐器组

examples: The economics lecture examines the long-term repercussions of the factory closures on the surrounding towns.

gloss_en: an indirect and usually bad consequence of an action or event

def_en: a small waterfall or series of small waterfalls

def_en: rush down in big quantities, like a cascade
arrange (open windows) on a computer desktop so that they overlap each other, with the title bars visible

gloss_en: a small waterfall, or a large amount of something falling or flowing downward.

def_en: a person who has fallen into a decadent state (morally or artistically)

def_en: marked by excessive self-indulgence and moral decay
examples: They ended the celebration with a decadent chocolate cake dripping with cream.

gloss_en: showing a decline in moral or artistic standards, or marked by excessive luxury and self-indulgence.

def_en: heedful of potential consequences

def_en: v surround so as to force to give up
v beat through cleverness and wit
v avoid or try to avoid fulfilling, answering, or performing (duties, questions, or issues)

gloss_en: to cleverly avoid or get around a rule, problem, or obstacle instead of dealing with it directly.

def_en: the act of deceiving

def_zh: 瞒骗, 诈欺, 欺骗

gloss_en: the act of deliberately making someone believe something that is not true.

def_en: causing one to believe what is not true or fail to believe what is true
designed to deceive or mislead either deliberately or inadvertently
def_zh: 迷惑的, 虚伪的, 欺诈的

gloss_en: likely to make someone believe something that is not true; misleading.

def_en: taking exception; especially a quibble based on a captious argument

def_zh: 说明, 字幕, 标题

def_zh: 加上标题, 加上说明

gloss_en: a short piece of text printed under a picture or across the screen to explain it or show what is being said.

def_en: informal or natural; especially caught off guard or unprepared
openly straightforward and direct without reserve or secretiveness
def_zh: 坦白的, 率直的, 公正的

gloss_en: honest and direct, saying what you really think.

gloss_en: the quality of being open, honest, and sincere in speech

def_en: a strip of land projecting into a body of water

def_zh: 岬, 海角, 披肩, 斗蓬

def_en: a precipitated solid substance in suspension or after settling or filtering
def_zh: 沉淀物, 凝结物, 结果

def_en: bring about abruptly
separate as a fine suspension of solid particles
fall from clouds
def_zh: 猛抛, 使陷入, 促成, 使沉淀

def_en: the quantity of water falling to earth at a specific place within a specified period of time
the process of forming a chemical precipitate
the falling to earth of any form of water (rain or snow or hail or sleet or mist)
def_zh: 坠落, 沉淀, 降水, 轻率, 急速, 猛冲, 突如其来

examples: The agriculture lecture notes that floriculture has become a major export industry in countries like the Netherlands and Kenya.

gloss_en: the branch of horticulture concerned with growing flowers as a commercial industry

examples: The marine biology lecture explained how modern aquaculture now supplies nearly half of the seafood people eat worldwide.

gloss_en: the farming of fish, shellfish, or aquatic plants under controlled conditions for food or commercial use

examples: She studied horticulture and now designs award-winning gardens for private clients.

def_en: n the cultivation of plants

def_en: a stopping

def_zh: 中止, 中断, 停止

def_zh: 特许, 让步, 认可

def_en: politely warm and friendly

def_zh: 热忱的, 诚恳的, 有兴奋作用的

gloss_en: relating to the heart or heart disease.

gloss_en: to destroy something completely by burning it

gloss_en: relating to ashes, especially the ashes of a cremated body

knowledge acquired by learning and instruction
the gradual process of acquiring knowledge
the profession of teaching (especially at a school or college or university)
def_zh: 教育, 训练, 教育学

gloss_en: to persuade or lead someone into doing something

examples: The history lecture explained how the assembly line made it possible to mass-produce cars at a low cost.

gloss_en: to manufacture large quantities of a product using standardized machinery and processes

gloss_en: the proportion of a population's members that successfully produce surviving offspring

def_en: easily influenced

def_en: s completely wanting or lacking

def_en: leave

gloss_en: relating to the sense of smell.
